target: data/wins.json WIN-010 — single targeted substring replacement rewriting the P7 "clean external test" paragraph to resolve ISS-1455 (asymmetric test setup: nine dome FSF numbers vs a vague "consistent with 30°–50° response zone" competitor) and ISS-1456 (implicit linear-coverage assumption baked into the residual definition without acknowledgement). The fix pins a specific mag-lat prediction method per station and adds a linear-coverage hedge applied symmetrically to both models.
patch_instructions: One substring replacement inside WIN-010.detail_verdict_text. The BEFORE string is the entire three-sentence P7 block that EXP-228 wrote (starts with "Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r..." and ends with "...something non-trivial will need explaining."). The AFTER string is a five-sentence rewrite that (a) adds an explicit pre-registered mag-lat prediction method — evaluate each station at its CGM magnetic latitude from the IAGA registry, use Kim & Chang (2018) 30°–50° response envelope as the ensemble-median/suppressed classifier, with an upgrade path to a smooth f(mag_lat) fit if binned ensemble amplitudes become available — and (b) acknowledges that the linear-coverage factor common to both predictions is a first-order approximation for the standard model (rigorous Sq treatment integrates through the ionospheric-conductivity structure) but is adopted symmetrically so the residual comparison does not bias either direction. No other WIN-010 fields are touched. BEFORE verified unique in current data/wins.json WIN-010.detail_verdict_text before writing this EXP.

patches.before: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), after factoring out the shadow-coverage term both models share — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude scaling consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. Both the dome formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF and a standard-physics prediction ≈ baseline × coverage × f(mag_lat) include coverage as a factor, so coverage is not the discriminator — the real fingerprint is geographic-latitude FSF versus magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat). We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track magnetic-latitude scaling more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats mag-lat scaling on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.after: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), under the simplest linear-coverage approximation applied identically to both models — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) profile consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. To pin the competing prediction down before 2026 data arrives, we will evaluate each of the nine stations at its CGM magnetic latitude (IAGA observatory registry) and use Kim & Chang (2018)'s published 30°–50° Y-component response envelope as a pre-registered reference: stations with magnetic latitude inside the band are predicted to carry an ensemble-median signal, stations outside are predicted to be suppressed — a crude but falsifiable nine-number competitor to the dome's nine FSF numbers. If the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available we will instead fit a smooth f(mag_lat) across the ensemble and evaluate it per station; either way the standard-model side produces specific per-station predictions, not a vibe. The linear-coverage form common to both predictions is the dome's explicit choice and a first-order approximation for the standard model — a rigorous Sq treatment enters coverage through the ionospheric-conductivity integral with altitude and local-time structure rather than as a bare multiplier — but we adopt it symmetrically so the residual comparison does not bias either direction; any nonlinear-coverage effect will drift both residuals in parallel, leaving the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint intact. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track the magnetic-latitude prediction more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats the magnetic-latitude prediction on this pre-registered nine-station test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.rationale: Resolves c7 Hole 1 (ISS-1455) by committing, in text, to a specific pre-registered magnetic-latitude prediction method before 2026 data arrives — the nine stations each get a mag-lat value from the IAGA/CGM registry, Kim & Chang (2018) 30°–50° envelope binarises into ensemble-median vs suppressed, with a smooth-fit upgrade path if ensemble binned amplitudes are obtainable. This turns the vague "consistent with the 30°–50° response zone" into nine specific predictions that match the dome's nine FSF numbers count-for-count, closing the "any 2026 outcome is consistent with Kim & Chang" loophole. Resolves c7 Hole 2 (ISS-1456) by naming the linear-coverage form as an approximation — the dome's explicit formulation AND a first-order rendering of the standard model, acknowledging the rigorous Sq treatment is more complex (ionospheric-conductivity integral with altitude and local-time structure). Applied symmetrically, the approximation biases both residuals equally; any nonlinear-coverage effect cancels in the comparison, leaving the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint as the real discriminator. This is the c7 Hole 2 recommendation option (a) — the hedge — rather than (b) — a literature confirmation that Kim & Chang endorses linear coverage — because the symmetric-approximation argument is self-contained; option (b) can be a follow-up micro-patch if/when a citation is identified.

holes_addressed.hole: ISS-1455 / c7 Hole 1 (moderate) — P7 asymmetric test setup
holes_addressed.description: Dome side has nine specific FSF numbers (0.642–2.075, listed in P7). Competitor side was described only as "consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone" — no per-station predictions. A dome defender could argue that whatever 2026 residual pattern appears, we can post-hoc pronounce it consistent with Kim & Chang because we never pinned the competitor down.
holes_addressed.fix: Commit in text to a specific pre-registered evaluation: each station's CGM magnetic latitude from the IAGA observatory registry, then the Kim & Chang (2018) 30°–50° response envelope used as a binary classifier (inside-band → ensemble-median signal; outside → suppressed). This produces nine specific numbers on the competitor side. Acknowledged as crude (binary inside/outside is not a smooth function), with an upgrade path: if the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available, we fit a smooth f(mag_lat) and evaluate per station. The prediction is now falsifiable against the dome's nine FSF numbers before 2026 data arrives — symmetric test setup.

holes_addressed.hole: ISS-1456 / c7 Hole 2 (moderate) — Implicit linear-coverage assumption in the residual definition
holes_addressed.description: The old P7 residual formulation ((observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage)) treats coverage as a bare multiplicative factor. The dome formula endorses this explicitly (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF). Rigorous Sq physics does not — coverage enters the ionospheric-conductivity integral with altitude and local-time structure, so linear coverage is only a first-order approximation on the standard-model side. By residualizing out coverage linearly, the old text implicitly adopts the dome's formulation for the standard model without saying so — a rigorous defender can argue we biased the residual toward the dome's functional form.
holes_addressed.fix: Name the linear-coverage form as an approximation on the standard-model side, acknowledge the rigorous Sq treatment (ionospheric-conductivity integral), and argue that applying the same approximation to both models symmetrically means nonlinear-coverage effects drift both residuals in parallel. The geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint is preserved — any coverage-nonlinearity moves both predictions together, not one relative to the other. This is c7 Hole 2 option (a) — the hedge — adopted in the text rather than deferred.

new_evidence_added: IAGA observatory registry (authoritative source of CGM magnetic latitudes) named as the lookup source for each of the nine 2026-08-12 stations — makes the competitor side concretely computable.

Kim & Chang (2018) 30°–50° Y-component response envelope explicitly re-cast as a binary classifier (inside-band → signal-present/ensemble-median amplitude; outside → suppressed) — makes the pre-registered competitor prediction falsifiable.
Upgrade path named: fit a smooth f(mag_lat) across Kim & Chang's 39-eclipse binned ensemble if binned amplitudes become available. No new citation needed; Kim & Chang (2018) is already in the WIN.

Explicit acknowledgement of rigorous Sq coverage treatment (ionospheric-conductivity integral with altitude and local-time structure) — adopted as framing, no new citation required beyond Kim & Chang (2018) and Chapman (1933) already present.

anticipated_objections.objection: A binary inside-band/outside-band classifier is much coarser than the dome's nine-valued FSF table. You have leveled the predictions unfairly — granting the dome a continuous amplitude prediction while the standard model gets thumbs-up/thumbs-down.
anticipated_objections.response: Granted — and we say so in the AFTER text: "crude but falsifiable." The binary classifier is the weakest pre-registered form that is still committed in writing before 2026 data arrives. The upgrade path (smooth f(mag_lat) fit from the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes) is named inline so readers see the planned refinement. The test itself is still symmetric in the thing that matters: both sides produce nine station-indexed predictions that can be ranked against observations. If the dome's nine continuous numbers beat the standard model's nine categorical numbers, that is a genuine scientific signal — but the dome defender can no longer claim we left the competitor unpinned.

anticipated_objections.objection: Most of the 2026-08-12 European stations sit inside 30°–50° magnetic latitude. A binary in-band/out-of-band classifier will barely discriminate across stations — you have produced nine numbers that are nearly all identical.
anticipated_objections.response: Correct for the European core — EBR, SPT, HAD, NGK, CLF, COI, and COIN-cluster stations are all in-band, so their predicted amplitudes collapse to the ensemble median. LER and ESK are marginal or outside (≳50° CGM). SNK is far south (≲37° CGM) and also well outside the band. That gives a small but nonzero across-station variance on the standard-model side — plus the overall amplitude calibration (does the ensemble median match the observed mean?) is a separate test. The cleaner discrimination is still through the smooth f(mag_lat) upgrade, which we commit to adopting if the ensemble data becomes accessible. A reader who reads the AFTER text will see that limitation stated, not hidden.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adopting the dome's linear-coverage form for the standard model "symmetrically" is a rhetorical move, not a physics move. If coverage really is nonlinear, your residualization divides out the wrong thing for the standard model, and the residual shape you see could still favor whichever model's approximation was closer to true.
anticipated_objections.response: A defensible worry, but the AFTER text engages it directly: nonlinear-coverage effects applied to both residuals drift them in parallel, because the coverage factor at each station is the same input regardless of which model divides through with it. What differs between the two residual patterns is the per-station factor left behind — geographic-latitude FSF for the dome, magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) for the standard model. The geographic-vs-magnetic fingerprint is preserved under any coverage transformation that is a function only of coverage (not of latitude). This is not air-tight if coverage-dependence interacts with latitude in an unknown way, but it is the correct first-order argument and the text names the approximation rather than hiding it.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why commit to the binary-classifier method now rather than waiting to see what ensemble data is reachable first?
anticipated_objections.response: The curmudgeon c7 Hole 1 recommendation was explicit: commit to a specific evaluation method BEFORE 2026 data arrives so no post-hoc reinterpretation is possible. If we wait and then adopt whichever method fits best, we give up the pre-registration advantage — the whole point of the "clean external test" framing. The binary classifier is the weakest concrete form; the smooth fit is a strict upgrade if ensemble access becomes available. Either way we have committed in public to a rule for pronouncing the competitor prediction before 2026-08-12.

kernel_of_truth: c7 curmudgeon is right on both holes. Hole 1: EXP-228 left the competitor under-specified because it was responding to EXP-220's FSF concession and focused on identifying the RIGHT discriminator (geographic vs magnetic latitude) rather than OPERATIONALIZING the competitor's prediction. That left a real gap a capable defender can exploit. Hole 2: linear coverage is baked into the dome's scaling equation explicitly and was adopted implicitly on the standard side because the residual formulation only works if both sides share a common multiplicative coverage term. We never named that shared assumption or its approximation status. Both are honest weaknesses in the EXP-228 rewrite.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_the_dome: Even after this patch, the absolute-magnitude observation-anchoring critique stands: the dome's −10.9 nT baseline is the very 2017 BOU observation it claims to predict, reused as the 2026 baseline input. And the FSF-shape-is-not-unique critique stands: any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent FSF of the same form (c6/c7 Hole 3, deferred to its own follow-up patch). This EXP sharpens the pre-registered 2026 test but does not alter the operative non-discrimination argument.
cross_win_consistency_flag: The magnetic-latitude-vs-geographic-latitude framing is now more sharply operationalized in WIN-010. If WIN-068 gets its own detail_verdict_text rewrite (currently only the finding field propagation is flagged), the sharpened operational definition in this patch should be mirrored into WIN-068 P3 to keep the Sq-mechanism cluster internally consistent. Not in scope for this EXP.
out_of_scope_deliberately: c7 Hole 2 option (b) — literature check for Kim & Chang (2018) or a follow-on paper endorsing linear-coverage scaling at mid-latitudes. Option (a) — the hedge — is adopted here; option (b) can be a follow-up micro-patch if/when a specific citation is identified.
c6/c7 Hole 3 (any-smooth-monotonic-H(r) claim in P3) — separate follow-up EXP per EXP-228 out_of_scope_deliberately list.
c6/c7 Hole 4 (0.1%/1.8% precision claim in P2 — actual distribution is 0.5%/2.2%) — separate micro-patch.
c6/c7 Hole 5 (Chapman 1933 overclaim elsewhere in text) — separate micro-patch.
c6/c7 Hole 6 (P3 sentence about H(r) being fitted to sun/moon altitude calibration targets) — separate additive micro-patch.
c6/c7 Hole 7 (OTS nuance in post-hoc paragraph) — separate additive micro-patch.

c7 stronger-arguments suggestion (precomputed per-station magnetic latitudes + Kim & Chang ensemble amplitudes table) — referenced here as the upgrade path but the full tabulation itself is follow-up work, possibly by a tinker or analyst fingerprint-hunt follow-up.
WIN-068 detail_verdict_text parallel patch — flagged as cross-WIN consistency concern; separate EXP.
propagation_note: Does not alter the finding field (EXP-228 already re-wrote it) or the tldr_verdict/tldr_evidence fields. The P7 paragraph is the only locus of change. If the decider observes TLDR drift from this edit, the TLDR remains layman-level — the pre-registered mag-lat method is detail-level content.
